answer:
**In Charles Dunoyer's *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, chapter 16 ("VI. De la liberté de l'industrie manufacturière"), the author warns of the harm caused by a lack of prudence in manufacturing, stating that "Dissipators are perhaps less harmful to it than overly adventurous speculators," as the latter discredit industry through imprudent examples.[^1] He illustrates this with the following anecdote:**

> A great Russian lord, known for the liberality of his sentiments, asked one of our chemists to teach him how to distill potatoes; he ardently desired, he said, to excite by his example the inhabitants of his lands to devote themselves to this kind of fabrication: for the rest, he declared *that he wished to make no profit from it, and that he would even consent to lose a great deal on it*. “God forbid,” replied the learned man, “that I should teach you an art in which you would consent to suffer losses; it is a poor service to industry to make such a use of one's means; the only enterprises whose example is favorable to it are those that are lucrative, and to form such ones, one must bring to them an attention and a prudence that one hardly takes the trouble to apply when one has neither the need, nor the desire to obtain profits.”[^2]

[^1]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [chapter 16](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-06b6eb).

[^2]: Charles Dunoyer, *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL II*, [paragraphs](/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-ii-d6ae03/16-vi-de-la-liberte-de-lindustrie-manufacturiere/translation/#p-06b6eb/#p-dd47d2).
